coredata.dc:title: Serendipity in solution–GQDs zeolitic imidazole frameworks nanocomposites for highly sensitive detection of sulfide ions
coredata.dc:creator: Sammi, Heena
Kukkar, Deepak
Singh, Jaskaran
Kukkar, Preeti
Kaur, Rajwinder
Kaur, Harmanpreet
Rawat, Mohit
Singh, Gurjinder
Kim, Ki-Hyun
coredata.dc:description: An innovative and facile sensing approach for common inorganic anions present in aqueous media has been developed using nanocomposites of europium doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs:Eu) with zeolitic imidazole frameworks (ZIF-8). GQDs:Eu, prepared initially by solvothermal approach (at <10nm in diameter), exhibited a strong and intense blue emission at 430nm when dispersed in ethanol. ZIF-8 nanocubes, synthesized on a parallel basis, were in a fairly uniform size distribution (e.g., 185nm in diameter). The formation of nanocomposites by surface adsorption (between GQDs:Eu and ZIF-8) led to significant increase in fluorescence intensity as observed through photo-luminescence spectroscopy. Their interactions with anions (e.g., in terms of variations in the fluorescence intensity) were initially checked against sulfide and other anionic species (e.g., Br−, Cl−, F−, and NO3 −). Among the tested ions, GQDs:Eu-ZIF-8 were found to effectively and sensitively detect sulfide (S2−) with significantly high turn on fluorescence. The feasibility of this nanosensing system was explored further toward the detection of S2− ions. These nanocomposites were thus demonstrated to be feasible for solution-based sensing of S2− ions with high reproducibility and sensitivity at limit of detection (LOD) of 0.12ppm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Graphene quantum dots
Pollutant
Sensing
Sulfide
Zeolitic imidazole frameworks

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQDs from graphene oxide (GO)
Synthesis of Eu doped GQDs (GQDs:Eu) from GO by solvothermal method
Characterization techniques and basic quality assurance (QA)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Government of Rajasthan

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by financial grant (No. YSS/2015/000212 ) received from SERB, Department of Science and Technology, Govt of India under the young scientist scheme. Author Ms. Preeti Kukkar acknowledges the support of SERB for providing Junior Research Fellowship. Authors are thankful to SGPC, Sri Amritsar for providing the necessary laboratory facilities. KHK acknowledges support made in part by grants from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning (No. 2016R1E1A1A01940995 ). Appendix A

The following is Supplementary data to this article: Er. Heena Sammi has completed M.Tech. in Nanotechnology at Department of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. She has keen interest and knowledge in chemical and biogenic synthesis of carbon based nanomaterials and their sensing applications. She further aims to pursue higher studies in the domain of carbon based nanomaterials for environmental sensing applications. Dr. Deepak Kukkar currently holds the position of Assistant Professor at Department of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. He did his M.Sc. and Ph.D. from Panjab University, Chandigarh. He has keen interest in synthesis of luminescent metal organic frameworks for sensing of pollutant species and theranostic applications. He has twelve publications in International Journals to his credit. Recently, he has also received financial assistance from Science and Engineering Research Board, DST, Govt. of India on development of nanosensor based upon luminescent metal organic frameworks for sensing and removal of pollutant pesticides. Er. Jaskaran Singh has completed his M.Tech. from Department of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. He aims to do innovative research work on Biocompatible Metal Organic Frameworks for therapeutic applications. Ms. Preeti Kukkar is currently pursuing Ph.D. in Chemistry from Department of Chemistry, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. Her research work is focused on synthesis of luminescent metal organic frameworks for sensing and removal of pollutant species from solution. Ms. Rajwinder Kaur has completed her B.Tech. in Nanotechnology from Department of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. She did her minor and major project under the supervision of Dr. Deepak Kukkar. She has been actively involved in synthesis of graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots in the laboratory. Ms. Harmanpreet Kaur is currently pursuing 9th semester of M.Tech. in Nanotechnology at Department of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. She is doing her minor and major project under the supervision of Dr. Deepak Kukkar. She is focusing on covalent conjugation of biomolecules with metal organic frameworks and subsequent sensing of analytes using these bioconjugates. Er. Mohit Rawat currently holds the position of Assistant Professor at Department of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. His major research interest include Synthesis and characterization of Biogenic Nanomaterials, Size and Shape controlled synthesis of metal, clay nanoparticles and their nanocomposites, Synthesis of Bimetallic (core-shell, inverted core-shell and alloyed) nanoparticles and Self-assembly of metal nanoparticles using different ligands and their application in environmental remediation. Er. Gurjinder Singh is currently Department Incharge and Assistant Professor at Department of Electronics Engineering, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. He is also pursuing Ph.D. in Nanoelectronics on High Surface Area Metal Oxides Nanomaterials for Electronics and Sensor Technology Applications. His research interest includes synthesis of semi-conductor nanoparticles for dye sensitized solar cell applications. Prof. Ki-Hyun Kim was at Florida State University for an M.S. (1984–1986) and at University of South Florida for a Ph.D. (1988–1992). He was a Research Associate at ORNL, USA (1992–1994). Then, he moved to Korea and stayed at Sang Ji University (1995–1998). In 1999, he joined Sejong University. In 2014, he moved to Dept. of Civil and Environmental Engineering at Hanyang University. His research areas focus on environmental analysis and air quality/human health management based on coordination polymers and other advanced materials. He was awarded a National Star Faculty in 2006. He is serving as an editorial member of several journals (e.g., Environmental Research, Atmospheric Pollution Research, Sensors, and Scientific World). He has published more than 480 SCI journal articles.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of synthesis procedure for highly luminescent Eu-doped GQDs by a one-step solvothermal approach.

UV–vis absorption spectra of undoped GQDs in DMF (Green) and GQDs:Eu (in ethanol (red) vs. water (blue)). The inset shows the presence of additional peak for GQDs:Eu at 637nm when dispersed in ethanol. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

PL spectra of undoped GQDs and GQDs:Eu dispersed in ethanol. (A) Excitation spectra of undoped GQDs and GQDs:Eu showing weaker and broad peak at 375nm and strong peak at 369nm, respectively and (B) Emission spectra of undoped GQDs, GQDs:Eu (prisitine), and GQDs:Eu (purified) showing weak band at 433, intense band at 435, and a band of maximum intensity at 435nm, respectively. Photograph in B shows increasing FL intensity of GQDs with doping and purification under UV light of 365nm.

TEM image showing: (A) cylindrical tube like structures upon solvothermal treatment of GO sheets for 5h, (B) formation of highly dispersed GQDs:Eu NPs with uniform size distribution; Inset of B shows SAED pattern image with indicated d spacings, and (C) HRTEM image of GQDs:Eu. Scale bars in A, B, and C correspond to 100nm, 1μm, and 5nm, respectively.

FTIR analysis of undoped GQDs (lower panel) and GQDs:Eu (upper panel). Presence of almost similar types of functional groups typical to strong OH absorption reflects the hydrophilic nature of GQDs (doped and undoped) along with solubility in polar solvents.

Morphological and structural characterization of ZIF-8 NCs: (A) Average diameter of ZIF-8 NCs with uniform size distribution. The mean diameter of the particles was 189.2nm with PDI value of 0.034, (B) TEM image of ZIF-8 NCs with uniform size distribution and cubical appearance, and (C) XRD pattern of ZIF-8 NCs showing characteristic sodalite structure of the NPs. Scale bar in the B corresponds to 500nm.

FTIR spectra of ZIF (lower panel) and GQDs:Eu-ZIF-8 nanocomposite (upper panel). Absence of characteristic carboxylic group peaks in the nanocomposite confirmed the adsorption of GQDs:Eu in ZIF-8 pores.

Schematic of S2− sensing by GQDs based on an indirect approach involving FL enhancement through the separation of GQDs in ZIF-8 matrix. Addition of S2− ions to GQDs-ZIF-8 solution resulted in further enhancement of GQDs FL intensity.

PL studies showing: (A) spectra of the GQDs:Eu NCs at varying volumes of ZIF-8 NCs and (B) Intensity of the emission maxima as a function of ZIF-8 NCs volume. FL intensity increased up to 120μl of ZIF-8 NCs and showed a sudden decrease upon raising the ZIF-8 NCs volume to 350μl.

PL spectra of the Eu:GQDs-ZIF-8 nanocomposite at excitation wavelength of 369nm after the addition of S2− and other anions. (A) Variation in FL intensity of composite as a function of wavelength and (B) Histogram presentation of relative variations in FL intensity of different anions at the emission maxima.

PL studies showing Eu:GQDs-ZIF-8 nanocomposite at excitation wavelength of 369nm after the addition of different volumes of S2− anion. (A) Variation in FL intensity of nanocomposite as a function of wavelength and (B) Plot of I0/I (at emission maxima) of the nanocomposites with the incremental addition of S2− ion solution.
Stern-Volmer quenching coefficients (Ksv) of various anions in relation to the luminescent intensity of Eu:GQDs-ZIF-8 nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Serendipity in solution–GQDs zeolitic imidazole frameworks nanocomposites for highly sensitive detection of sulfide ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib, 140406, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib, 140406, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronics Engineering, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib, 140406, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil & Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-Ro, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib, 140406, Punjab, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An innovative and facile sensing approach for solution-based sensing of sulfide (S2−) anions using nanocomposites of europium doped Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs:Eu) with Zeolitic Imidazole Frameworks (ZIF-8) has been reported. These nanocomposites are thus demonstrated as an efficient platform for solution-based sensing of S2− ions with high reproducibility and selectivity.

An innovative and facile sensing approach for common inorganic anions present in aqueous media has been developed using nanocomposites of europium doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs:Eu) with zeolitic imidazole frameworks (ZIF-8). GQDs:Eu, prepared initially by solvothermal approach (at <10nm in diameter), exhibited a strong and intense blue emission at 430nm when dispersed in ethanol. ZIF-8 nanocubes, synthesized on a parallel basis, were in a fairly uniform size distribution (e.g., 185nm in diameter). The formation of nanocomposites by surface adsorption (between GQDs:Eu and ZIF-8) led to significant increase in fluorescence intensity as observed through photo-luminescence spectroscopy. Their interactions with anions (e.g., in terms of variations in the fluorescence intensity) were initially checked against sulfide and other anionic species (e.g., Br−, Cl−, F−, and NO3 −). Among the tested ions, GQDs:Eu-ZIF-8 were found to effectively and sensitively detect sulfide (S2−) with significantly high turn on fluorescence. The feasibility of this nanosensing system was explored further toward the detection of S2− ions. These nanocomposites were thus demonstrated to be feasible for solution-based sensing of S2− ions with high reproducibility and sensitivity at limit of detection (LOD) of 0.12ppm.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are one of the recently emerging nanomaterials with the potential for use as a stable and sensitive platform to detect pollutants in solution phase [1]. Structurally, they are fragments of graphene sheet with size-tunable band gap characteristics compatible with inorganic quantum dots. As such, GQDs have continuously been gaining the scientific attention owing to their small size, tunable luminescent properties, biocompatibility, relatively low toxicity, and ease of functionalization [2,3]. They also exhibit size-dependent tunable band gap, strong quantum confinement, and edge effects compared to other graphene structures [4–7]. The most attractive property of GQDs is that they are carbon material with sufficient availability. The presence of multiple functional groups at the surface allows GQDs to form stable dispersions in aqueous media and many other solvents. In addition, small size of GQDs along with enhanced surface functionality allows them to be easily introduced into larger matrices for the formation of nanocomposites with enhanced functional capabilities [8,9].

Porous nanomaterials called metal organic framework (MOFs) are one of the preferable platforms for preparing functional nanocomposites owing to their unique properties such as hybrid structural composition, permanent porosity, and tunable surface functionalities [10–12]. Among various types of MOFs, Zeolitic Imidazole Frameworks (ZIF-8) MOFs are highly appropriate matrices for nanocomposite formation with GQDs owing to their distinct advantages (e.g., porous appearance, large cavities, high stability, facile synthesis, and benign nature) [13,14]. Their porous matrix can accommodate smaller particles to yield composite nanostructures with better functional capabilities [15]. The nanocomposite formed by GQDs and MOFs may exhibit highly luminescent properties which are sensitive enough for the detection of trace-level analytes present in solution.

In light of these distinguishing features of GQDs and ZIF-8, we propose the synthesis of nanocomposite consisting of europium (Eu) doped GQDs (GQDs:Eu) and ZIF-8 particles to demonstrate its feasibility for the sensitive detection of sulfide (S2−) ions in ethanol media. S2− ions are released from agriculture, petroleum, and sewage processing industries in the form of H2S [16]. It is also used as a fungicide for increased growth of pears and apples [17]. The intracellular concentrations of S2− ions, as a component of mitochondrial electron transport chain (ETC), are generally observed in the range of 0.01–1.0μM [18]. At about 3–30 times higher concentrations, S2− inhibits cytochrome C oxidase of the ETC, thereby disrupting ATP synthesis in mitochondria [16,17]. Uncontrolled exposure to high concentrations of S2− ions has been reported to cause neurological disorders. In addition, it is also well known by the potential to induce physiological toxicity stress in the organisms from aquatic ecosystems and wetlands [19]. Under the waterlogging conditions, S2− concentrations can reach up to 15mmolL−1 in marine sediments, thereby causing oxidative stress (e.g., leading to phytotoxicity) [19].

In view of these life threatening disorders, our work has focused on the development of highly sensitive detection platform for S2− anions based on GQDs:Eu-ZIF-8 nanocomposites. The approach employed in this study is based on the enhancement of the fluorescence (FL) intensity of GQDs by forming nanocomposites with ZIF-8 nanocubes (NCs) through a host guest interaction. To test the feasibility of the nanocomposites in such respects, their response behavior was examined after being exposed to sodium salts of S2− along with other potential interfering anionic species (S2−, F−, Br−, Cl−, and NO3 −). Among these anions, S2− exhibited continuously strongest turn on FL behavior. In contrast, most anions were generally subject to turn off FL phenomenon under identical experimental conditions. The proposed nanocomposites were thus turned out to efficiently sense ultra-low concentrations of S2− anions below environmentally permissible levels. The results of this study were thus evaluated to determine the feasibility of the proposed FL-based tunable nanocomposites (GQDs:Eu-ZIF-8) as a sensing platform for highly sensitive detection of anionic pollutant species, especially S2−.

All the reagents used in the current experimental study were of analytical grade unless otherwise stated. Briefly, graphite fine powder and NaF were purchased from Central Drug House (India). Europium chloride (III) hexahydrate (EuCl3·6H2O) was purchased from Sigma-aldrich, USA. N,N′-Dimethyl formamide (DMF), NaNO3, KMnO4, H2O2, H2SO4, Na2S, NaBr, NaNO3, and NaCl were purchased from Merck, Germany. Deionized (DI) water of 18.2 MΩ.cm resistivity was used throughout all the experiments, wherever required.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs from graphene oxide (GO)
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (GO) was used as a precursor for GQDs fabrication in the current study. It was synthesized according to hummer’s method [20] with slight modifications as described in supporting information. Synthesis of GQDs from GO was carried out by one step solvothermal approach. Briefly, 0.27g GO was precisely weighed, added in 20ml DMF and allowed to sonicate for 45min. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to teflon lined vessel and heated in an oven at 200°C for 5–7h. After completion of the reaction, the vessel was cooled down to room temperature. The final product containing black precipitates was washed with ethanol and filtered using a 1.5μm micro porous membrane under reduced pressure. The filtrate was collected and analyzed to confirm the formation of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Eu doped GQDs (GQDs:Eu) from GO by solvothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: Addition of dopant materials to GQDs accounts for their enhanced photo-luminescent properties. For proof of concept, Eu doped GQDs (GQDs:Eu) were synthesized according to the similar solvothermal approach discussed earlier and schematically presented in Fig. 1 . In a typical experiment, 0.27g GO was added in 15ml DMF in the first step and kept in an ultrasonic bath for 30min. In a parallel experiment, 0.005g EuCl3·6H2O was added to 5ml DMF and allowed to sonicate for 5min. As prepared EuCl3·6H2O solution was added to the dispersed GO solution and again sonicated for 15min. The solution was subsequently transferred to teflon lined vessel and heated at 200°C for 5–7h and treated in the same manner as that of the undoped GQDs. Both GQDs and GQDs:Eu were further purified by gel permeation chromatography performed with sephacryl S-200 column to remove unreacted species and aggregates.

Prior to sensing of anions, nanocomposite formation between GQDs:Eu and ZIF-8 was carried out by physical adsorption approach. Typically, 1.5ml of GQDs:Eu and varying volumes (100–350μl) of pre synthesized ZIF-8 NCs (10ppm) were mixed together and allowed for shaking in orbital shaker for 10–15min. The nanocomposite was obtained by ultracentrifugation and washed with ethanol for three-four times to remove unreacted species. Photo luminescence (PL) spectra of the obtained nanocomposites were recorded to check the variation in their FL intensity.

For sensing applications, 200μl of solutions containing S2− and other potential interfering anions (1ppm: viz. Na2S, NaBr, NaNO3, NaCl, and NaF) were added individually to the pristine nanocomposite solutions (1.85ml) and sonicated (5min). Subsequently, the solution was incubated for 15min. The FL spectra of as prepared samples were recorded by excitation at 369nm to check the effect of respective anions on the PL intensity of the nanocomposites. Unlike other tested anions, S2− ion exhibited the most distinctive turn on FL behavior while others generally exhibited turn off FL behavior (except bromide). In light of this distinctive turn on pattern, S2− was selected as the main target of this study for further sensing experiments.

In view of the distinct behavior of S2− ion, variation in nanocomposite FL intensity was studied as a function of S2− volume to check the sensitivity of our probe at lower volumes. Different volumes (0–600μl) of Na2S (1ppm) solution were sequentially added to the 1.85ml initial volume of nanocomposite solution as shown in Table S1 (Supporting Information; SI). The solution was sonicated each time for 5min and then incubated for 30min followed by washing with ethanol to remove unbound reactants. Finally, the FL spectra were recorded to evaluate the variation in the PL intensity of the nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization techniques and basic quality assurance (QA)
ce:section.ce:para: Samples for optical analysis were prepared in ethanol and analyzed in the range of 200–800nm. Absorption spectra were recorded on a research grade single monochromatic Shimadzu/UV-2600 spectrophotometer fitted with double beam optics. Molecular Structure of the GQDs and nanocomposites was determined on Bruker Alpha Fourier transforms infrared spectro-photometer operated in Attenuated Total Reflectance (ATR) mode in the range between 4000–400cm−1.

PL spectra were recorded on Edinburg FLS980 spectrophotometer supplied with computer-controlled, triple grating turret monochromators, with up to three different gratings permanently fitted in the wavelength range of 200–800nm. Ozone free 480W Xe lamp that covers a range of 280–1000nm was used as light source in the equipment. Surface morphology of the NPs was also determined using Carl Zeiss Supra 55 Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM) model operated at 10KV accelerating voltage. Samples for FE-SEM analysis were taken in powdered form and spread on a Si wafer pre-cleaned with Piranha solution and DI water followed by drying under vacuum. Si wafer immobilized with the sample were mounted on metallic stub with double sided carbon tape and sputter coated with gold for 60s at a rate of 0.5mA/sec. Micrographs were captured and the microcapsule size distribution was determined according to a reference scale.

Samples for Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) were prepared in Ethanol and mounted on a carbon coated grid and analyzed at 200KV accelerating voltage in Philips CM 200 model with a maximum resolution of 2.4A°. High Resolution TEM (HRTEM) analysis was carried on Jeol JEM 2100 microscope operated at conditions similar to TEM analysis.

To substantiate our observations, a quantitative analysis of anions was further performed to test the sensitivity of GQDs:Eu-ZIF-8 nanocomposites. Experimental data were fitted to the Stern-Volmer relationship as shown in Eq. (1) [21].
(1) Io/I=1+Ksv[M]
Where Io and I are the fluorescence intensities recorded in the absence and presence of anions, respectively, [M] is the concentration of anions in ppm, and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. The value of Ksv after the addition of different anions is presented in Table 1 .

The limit of detection (LOD) was also determined to check the effect of signal/noise (S/N) ratio on FL intensity [21]. The emission intensity of nanocomposites without any analyte was measured for 5 times and the standard deviation of blank measurements was determined to determine the S/N ratio. The LOD was subsequently calculated according to the following equation [21].
LOD=3δ/Ksv
Where δ is the standard deviation of blank measurements, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant.

In this research, the fabrication of highly luminescent GQDs was carried out from pre-synthesized GO sheets by solvothermal approach (Figs. S1–S3 in SI). UV–vis spectra of undoped GQDs and GQDs:Eu (purified and unpurified) dispersed in ethanol are shown in Fig. 2 . Both types of GQDs were characterized by a sharp absorption band at 218nm as evident from the Figure. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs depends upon the size, shape, and extraction medium. In our study, GQDs were prepared in DMF medium, where the absorption peak at 218nm reflects the π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds. The absorption shoulder at 278nm (magnified part in Fig. 2) can be assigned to the n-π* transition of the CO bonds. This peak becomes stronger with doping and further purification, as depicted in Fig. 2. In addition, the band gap energy corresponding to this peak is approximately 4.51eV, which is in accordance with that for graphene sheets. Similar observations made by other authors support the feasibility of our UV–vis absorption studies [21–24]. However, GQDs extracted in water are characterized by a strong absorption peak around 250nm with shoulder peaks in the range of 320–350nm [21]. This illustration clearly indicates the effect of reaction parameters upon the band gap properties of GQDs. In addition, the absence of Eu characteristic peak in the absorption spectra can be possibly attributed to the low concentration of the metal atom (i.e., Eu) in the GQDs.

The FL patterns of GQDs (undoped and doped with Eu) were explored by PL spectroscopy as presented in Fig. 3 . The excitation spectrum of GQDs (red line; 3A) was characterized by a relatively broad peak at 375nm. However GQDs:Eu (black line; 3A) exhibited an intense excitation band at 369nm. This doping dependent phenomenon was also well reflected in the emission spectra of both types of GQDs and shown in Fig. 3B. Undoped GQDs revealed broad emission spectrum along with weaker emission intensities in the blue region as evident from the emission maxima value of 433nm. However GQDs:Eu showed a relatively strong emission spectra with 2nm bathochromic shift at 435nm which further transformed into narrow emission spectra upon purification with column chromatography. The increase in emission intensities was well supported by change in the brightness of samples illuminated by UV light as depicted by the photographs in Fig. 3B. The strong emission intensity of GQDs (undoped and doped) can also be assigned to use of ethanol as dispersion medium in the current study. According to DLVO theory, relatively low dielectric constant of ethanol accounts for enhanced energy barrier between two interacting particles, thereby resulting in decreased agglomeration of GQDs [20,21]. This passivated surface defect explains the high FL intensity of GQDs in ethanol medium. Thus, it was clearly observed that doping with Eu and subsequent dispersion in ethanol medium account for formation of highly fluorescent GQDs with emission maxima in blue region. Additionally, the GQDs were highly stable as negligible variation in FL intensity was observed even after 4 months of dispersion in ethanol medium (Supporting information; Fig. S4).

Morphology and size distribution of GQDs:Eu was determined using TEM analysis and presented in Fig. 4 . Morphology of GQDs was critically dependent upon the duration of the reaction. When GO solution was solvothermally treated for 5h rod shaped structures were observed in addition to spherical particles as evident from TEM image in Fig. 4A. However, when the reaction was carried out for 7h only well dispersed spherical particles with spherical morphology, average size in the range between 10 and 20nm and uniformity in size distribution were observed (Fig. 4B). Thus optimization of GQDs:Eu synthesis required reaction to be carried out in excess of 5h. d spacings of GQDs:Eu calculated from selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (inset of Fig. 4B) were determined to be in the range from 0.1003 to 0.197nm. HRTEM image of GQDs:Eu shown in Fig. 4B depicts their high crystalline nature and diameter of around 10nm. Thus, the electron microscopy studies established formation of GQDs:Eu.

Molecular structures of GQDs and GQDs:Eu were ascertained by FTIR spectroscopy as presented in Fig. 5 . The weaker bands at 1694 and 1652cm−1 should be attributed to CO stretching of carboxyl groups in GQDs (lower panel) [22,25]. In comparison to the undoped GQDs, the presence of a stronger band at 1665cm−1 indicated the abundance of carboxyl groups (in the upper panel of Fig. 5). In addition, the lower panel in Fig. 5 indicates the presence of absorption bands characteristic of GQDs (e.g., at 3741, 3672, 3612, and 3566cm−1) due to the OH stretching of water molecules [22,23,25]. Similar bands at 3426 and 3184cm−1 were also observed for GQDs:Eu. The bands at 1521 and 1410cm−1 can be ascribed to CC stretching of aromatic compounds [21,24]. In case of GQDs:Eu, the aromatic bands were observed at around 1519 and 1400cm−1. In addition, the bands at 1174, 1067, and 1000cm−1 indicate the possible contribution of CO stretching [22,23,25]. The presence of two new peaks in the spectrum of GQDs:Eu (at 2939 and 854cm−1) should be ascribed to CH stretching from alkanes [24]. The presence of strong bands in the OH absorption region should be associated with the hydrophilicity and solubility of GQDs (doped and undoped) in polar solvents. Identification of all these bands thus supports the formation of carboxyl functionalized GQDs.

ZIF-8 NCs were synthesized by a facile one step mixing of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 2-methyl imidazole in an ultrasonicator bath through slight modifications in an earlier reported study [26]. Average size of the particles was determined by particle size analyzer and presented in Fig. 6 A. The average diameter of the particles was found to be 189.8nm with poly dispersity index (PDI) value of 0.034 as depicted in Fig. 6A. Appearance of single peak in the Figure demonstrated uniform size distribution of the particles. Thus, the particle size analyzer studies confirmed the formation of ZIF-NCs with uniform size distribution.

Transmission Electron micrograph (TEM) shown in Fig. 6B demonstrate uniform size distribution of the particles along with cubical appearance. The size of the particles as shown in the image was approximately 185nm. Thus, the TEM results were in agreement with the particle size analyzer studies and confirmed the formation of ZIF-8 NCs. Crystal structure of the ZIF-8 NCs was determined further by x-ray diffractometry (XRD) and presented in Fig. 6C. The well-defined sharp peaks obtained confirmed the highly crystalline nature of synthesized material. The characteristic reflection was found at 2θ=17.98°, attributed to the (222) peak of ZIF-8 NCs. The overall XRD patterns with peaks (corresponding to (112), (022), (013), (222), (114), (223), (131), and (044)) were in good agreement with previous reports, confirming the sodalite structure of ZIF-8 [27].

Nanocomposite formation between GQDs:Eu and ZIF-8 NCs was studied by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 7 ). Lower panel in the Figure shows the FTIR spectra of ZIF-8 NCs, where peaks at 3420 and 3133cm−1 correspond to NH stretching, while the one at 1664cm−1 reflected CO stretching of amide. Two peaks at 1584 and 1510cm−1 were due to NH bending of amides. The peaks from 1308 to 1098cm−1 were due to CN bending of amines and at 759, 692cm−1 were due to NH waging of amines. The observation of these peaks confirmed that amine groups present on ZIF-8 NCs. When the ZIF-8 NCs were added to the GQDs:Eu it resulted in disappearance of all peaks characteristic to carboxylic groups of GQDs discussed earlier. However the characteristic peaks of ZIF-8 NCs at 3292, 3630, 1566, 1016 and 868–678cm−1 were still observed (upper panel) to validate the presence of amine and hydroxyl groups. All these observations confirmed the formation of nanocomposite between GQDs:Eu and ZIF-8 NCs.

Nanocomposite formation between GQDs and ZIF-8 NCs may alter their FL behavior owing to the host guest interaction between these species. This altered FL pattern may be utilized in sensing of pollutant species. This hypothesis was subjected to analysis in the current study which involved F− mediated FL enhancement of GQDs-ZIF-8 nanocomposite. In a recent study by Lin et al. encapsulation of carbon QDs in ZIF-8 NCs results in high FL quantum yields and enhanced selectivity of carbon QDs for sensing of Cu+2 ions in solution [28]. However, their study relied upon in-situ encapsulation of carbon QDs in ZIF-8 NCs resulting in protection and increase FL intensity of the QDs. Based upon the schematic depicted in Fig. 8 , it is proposed that the formation of nanocomposites (between GQDs:Eu and ZIF-8 NCs) led to a better separation and dispersion of GQDs:Eu NCs owing to the host guest interaction caused by matrix structure of ZIF-8 NCs. This separation of GQDs resulted in the enhancement of FL intensities to account for serendipitous interaction between GQDs and ZIF-8 NCs. The FL intensity increases until the concentration of ZIF-8 NCs reaches a saturation level wherein FL intensity decreases with light scattering after the peak concentration of ZIF-8 NCs.

For proof of concept ZIF-8, NCs were added to GQDs:Eu solution in increasing volumes, change in FL intensities of GQDs were determined and presented in Fig. 9 . Fig. 9A shows variation in GQDs:Eu FL intensity as a function of ZIF-8 NCs volume with emission maxima as 432±3nm. When the emission maxima values were plotted against the volume of ZIF-8 NCs (8B), the FL intensity increased up to 120μl volume after that showed a saturation up to 320μl and finally decreased upon increasing the volume up to 350μl. The nanocomposites with 350μl of ZIF-8 NCs were subsequently used for S2− sensing. Accordingly, the decreased FL intensity of GQDs would provide a better sensitivity for S2− detection in solution.

To explore the FL sensing application of GQDs:Eu-ZIF-8 nanocomposites, the PL measurements were performed in the presence of various potential interfering substances (i.e., all anionic species (in 1ppm) such as S2− , Br− , NO3 − , Cl−, and F−). Fig. 10 confirms that the presence of different anions would lead to various influences on the PL intensity. The emission maxima of the nanocomposite were seen at 432nm (Fig. 10A). The emission intensity of composite increased for S2− and Br− anions, whereas it exhibited the reduction for other ions such as NO3 −, Cl− and F−. Fig. 10B shows the histogram presentation with relative FL intensity profile (F/F0) of nanocomposite in the presence of other competing anions tested in the experiment. Among all the tested anions, the lowest F/F0 value was shown by F− ions to indicate a maximum reduction in FL intensity by 11%. However, with the addition of S2−, nanocomposites exhibited the highest F/F0 value with a maximum enhancement (21%) in FL intensity among all tested anions. This observation implies that the proposed nanocomposite responded to a minimum of 1ppm concentration of all the anions. It must be noted that the turn on effect of nanocomposites was observed from both S2− and Br− only. However, that for S2− was very distinctive (e.g., over Br− ion), as depicted in Fig. 10B. On the other hand, the addition of other anions (e.g., F−, Cl−, and NO3 −) led to turn off FL behavior consistently.

The Ksv values of nanocomposites after the addition of S2− and Br- ions were found to be −1.7×104 and −3.4×103 ppm−1 respectively, thus yielding 5-fold increase in the sensitivity. Conversely, the calculated Ksv values for NO3 −, Cl−, and F− anions were found to be 4.9×103, 4.1×103, and 6.4×103 ppm−1, respectively. These values demonstrated turn off FL effect of these ions on the nanocomosites. The relatively similar Ksv values for these ions suggests that the turn off FL behavior of these anions should be highly comparable to each other. It also implies that although F− anions caused a maximum depreciation in the FL intensity of the nanocomposites, yet the presence of competing anions may interfere with the sensitivity of the PL measurements in environmental samples. However, the nanocomposites can be used as a highly sensitive and selective platform for detection of S2− ions owing to its distinctively high turn on FL intensity along with high Ksv value. In addition, the calculated Ksv values were found to be higher than those reported in earlier literature, thereby evidently demonstrating high sensitivity of our nanocomposite system [29–33].

The interaction of S2− ions to the nanocomposite solution counteracted the stirring effect and light scattering with increasing volume of ZIF-8 solution. It also facilitates further separation of GQDs, thereby resulting in volume independent turn on FL phenomenon (Fig. 11 ). To check the LOD, variation in the FL intensity was monitored with the addition of different volumes of 1ppm concentration of S2− ions. As shown in Fig. 11(A), the FL intensity increased with increasing volume of S2− solution up to 600μl across the measured wavelength range. To assess the dilution effect, relative FL intensity (I0/I) for S2− treated nanocomposites was plotted as a function of added S2− volumes (Fig. 11B). A linear decrease in the I0/I value was observed in the experimental range of 0–600μl volume of S2− solution, thus suggesting the high sensitivity of the nanocomposites towards S2− ions. The LOD value was accordingly calculated as 0.12ppm, thereby conforming to their enhanced sensitivity toward S2− sensing.

In this research, we report the feasibility of GQDs:Eu-ZIF-8 nanocomposites as a highly sensitive platform for the detection of S2− pollutants in ethanol medium. The first step involved synthesis of highly luminescent GQDs:Eu from pre-synthesized GO layers by a simple one step solvothermal approach and subsequent purification by gel permeation chromatography. In the next step, ZIF-8 NCs were added incrementally to GQDs:Eu solution to synthesize GQDs:Eu-ZIF-8 nanocomposites. Afterwards, their sensing potential for S2− and other anions (such as Br− , NO3 − , Cl−, F−, and NO3 −) was explored from a series of experiments to check their selectivity. The sensor showed volume independent turn on FL behavior upon the addition of S2− at 1ppm concentration. In view of these observations, this report portrays GQDs:Eu-ZIF-8 nanocomposites as highly sensitive and selective platform for the detection of S2− anionic analytes in ethanol medium. In near future, we are planning to apply this technique to the detection of sulfur ions from various environmental samples (e.g., pesticides) for the expansion of its feasibility.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by financial grant (No. YSS/2015/000212) received from SERB, Department of Science and Technology, Govt of India under the young scientist scheme. Author Ms. Preeti Kukkar acknowledges the support of SERB for providing Junior Research Fellowship. Authors are thankful to SGPC, Sri Amritsar for providing the necessary laboratory facilities. KHK acknowledges support made in part by grants from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning (No. 2016R1E1A1A01940995).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.09.129.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photo-Fenton reaction of graphene oxide: a new strategy to prepare graphene quantum dots for DNA cleavage
Nanomaterials and nanoparticles: sources and toxicity
Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue graphene quantum dots
Solution-processable graphene quantum dots as light absorbers for photovoltaics
Graphene at the edge: stability and dynamics
Synthesis of Stable colloidal graphene quantum dots with tunable size
Layered graphene quantum dots for photovoltaic devices
The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective
Engineering graphene/quantum dot interfaces for high performance electrochemical nanocomposites in detecting puerarin
Metal-organic framework materials with ultrahigh surface areas: is the sky the limit?
Metal-organic frameworks as sensory materials and imaging agents
Metal-organic frameworks a rapidly growing class of versatile nanoporous materials
Synthesis and characterization of ZIF-8 nanoparticles for controlled release of 6-mercaptopurine drug
Control of pore size and functionality in isoreticular zeolitic imidazolate frameworks and their carbon dioxide selective capture properties
Hydrogen sulfide-mechanisms of toxicity and development of an antidote
Testing the selectivity of pesticide effects on natural enemies in laboratory bioassays
H2S and its role in redox signaling
Sulfide as a soil phytotoxin—a review
A simple approach to stepwise synthesis of graphene oxide nanomaterials
The Tb(III) postsynthetic functional coordination polymer coatings on ZnO micronano arrays and application in small molecule sensing
Graphene quantum dots from graphite by liquid exfoliation showing excitation-independent emission, fluorescence upconversion and delayed fluorescence
Facile synthesis and photoluminescence mechanism of graphene quantum dots
Solvothermal method to prepare graphene quantum dots by hydrogen peroxide
Post-oxidation treated graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of copper ions
One-pot synthesis of protein-embedded metal-organic frameworks with enhanced biological activities
Formation of high crystalline ZIF-8 in an aqueous solution
Encapsulation of strongly fluorescent carbon quantum dots in metal-organic frameworks for enhancing chemical sensing
Carbon-dot-based fluorescent turn-on sensor for selectively detecting sulfide anions in totally aqueous media and imaging inside live cells
A Fe3O4@SiO2@graphene quantum dot core-shell structured nanomaterial as a fluorescent probe and for magnetic removal of mercury(II) ion
A hydrolytically stable luminescent cationic metal-organic framework for highly sensitive and selective sensing of chromate anion in natural water systems
Highly effective heterogeneous chemosensors of luminescent silica@coordination polymer core-shell micro-structures for metal ion sensing
Sensing of fluoride ions in aqueous media using a luminescent coordination polymer and liposome composite

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Er. Heena Sammi has completed M.Tech. in Nanotechnology at Department of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. She has keen interest and knowledge in chemical and biogenic synthesis of carbon based nanomaterials and their sensing applications. She further aims to pursue higher studies in the domain of carbon based nanomaterials for environmental sensing applications.

Dr. Deepak Kukkar currently holds the position of Assistant Professor at Department of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. He did his M.Sc. and Ph.D. from Panjab University, Chandigarh. He has keen interest in synthesis of luminescent metal organic frameworks for sensing of pollutant species and theranostic applications. He has twelve publications in International Journals to his credit. Recently, he has also received financial assistance from Science and Engineering Research Board, DST, Govt. of India on development of nanosensor based upon luminescent metal organic frameworks for sensing and removal of pollutant pesticides.

Er. Jaskaran Singh has completed his M.Tech. from Department of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. He aims to do innovative research work on Biocompatible Metal Organic Frameworks for therapeutic applications.

Ms. Preeti Kukkar is currently pursuing Ph.D. in Chemistry from Department of Chemistry, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. Her research work is focused on synthesis of luminescent metal organic frameworks for sensing and removal of pollutant species from solution.

Ms. Rajwinder Kaur has completed her B.Tech. in Nanotechnology from Department of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. She did her minor and major project under the supervision of Dr. Deepak Kukkar. She has been actively involved in synthesis of graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots in the laboratory.

Ms. Harmanpreet Kaur is currently pursuing 9th semester of M.Tech. in Nanotechnology at Department of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. She is doing her minor and major project under the supervision of Dr. Deepak Kukkar. She is focusing on covalent conjugation of biomolecules with metal organic frameworks and subsequent sensing of analytes using these bioconjugates.

Er. Mohit Rawat currently holds the position of Assistant Professor at Department of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. His major research interest include Synthesis and characterization of Biogenic Nanomaterials, Size and Shape controlled synthesis of metal, clay nanoparticles and their nanocomposites, Synthesis of Bimetallic (core-shell, inverted core-shell and alloyed) nanoparticles and Self-assembly of metal nanoparticles using different ligands and their application in environmental remediation.

Er. Gurjinder Singh is currently Department Incharge and Assistant Professor at Department of Electronics Engineering, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib. He is also pursuing Ph.D. in Nanoelectronics on High Surface Area Metal Oxides Nanomaterials for Electronics and Sensor Technology Applications. His research interest includes synthesis of semi-conductor nanoparticles for dye sensitized solar cell applications.

Prof. Ki-Hyun Kim was at Florida State University for an M.S. (1984–1986) and at University of South Florida for a Ph.D. (1988–1992). He was a Research Associate at ORNL, USA (1992–1994). Then, he moved to Korea and stayed at Sang Ji University (1995–1998). In 1999, he joined Sejong University. In 2014, he moved to Dept. of Civil and Environmental Engineering at Hanyang University. His research areas focus on environmental analysis and air quality/human health management based on coordination polymers and other advanced materials. He was awarded a National Star Faculty in 2006. He is serving as an editorial member of several journals (e.g., Environmental Research, Atmospheric Pollution Research, Sensors, and Scientific World). He has published more than 480 SCI journal articles.
